title: Desktop app crashes on save when task list contains non-ASCII characters from CSV import
problem: The desktop app crashes immediately when saving a task list that contains tasks imported from a CSV file. The CSV-imported tasks contain non-ASCII characters (smart quotes, em dashes, and similar typographic characters from spreadsheet software). A brief error dialog referencing 'encoding' flashes before the app force-closes. The user loses all unsaved work. Saving works correctly for lists without imported data or after manually removing imported tasks.
root_cause_hypothesis: The save/serialization path does not correctly handle non-ASCII characters (e.g., Unicode smart quotes U+201C/U+201D, em dashes U+2014) when writing task data to disk or to the backend. Likely the save function assumes ASCII-safe input or uses an encoding (e.g., Latin-1) that cannot represent the full range of characters introduced by CSV import, causing an unhandled encoding exception that crashes the app.
reproduction_steps: Prepare a CSV file containing tasks with typographic characters (smart quotes, em dashes, curly apostrophes — characters typical of spreadsheet/word processor output)
Import the CSV into TaskFlow to create a task list
Ensure the list has a substantial number of tasks (~200, though the trigger is likely the character content, not the count)
Observe: an encoding-related error dialog flashes briefly and the app crashes/closes
environment: macOS Sonoma, TaskFlow desktop app version 2.3.x
impact: Any user who imports task data from CSV (or other external sources containing non-ASCII characters) is completely unable to save their work. The user is blocked with no viable workaround short of manually re-entering all imported data. Data loss occurs on every save attempt.
recommended_fix: Investigate the save/serialization codepath in the desktop app. Ensure all text encoding uses UTF-8 throughout the pipeline — from in-memory representation through file/network serialization. Specifically check: (1) the file writer or API serializer used on save, (2) any intermediate string encoding conversions, (3) the CSV import path to see if it correctly tags imported text as UTF-8. Add defensive handling so encoding errors surface a user-friendly error message rather than crashing the app.
proposed_test_case: Create a task list containing tasks with smart quotes (“ ”), em dashes (—), accented characters (é, ñ), and emoji. Save the list and verify it persists correctly. Reload and verify characters are preserved. Also test with a large list (200+ tasks) containing mixed ASCII and non-ASCII content to rule out any size-dependent buffer issues.
information_gaps: Which specific characters in the CSV trigger the crash (developer can reproduce with any smart quotes/em dashes)
Whether the issue also exists on Windows/Linux or is macOS-specific
Exact text of the encoding error dialog (flashes too fast for user to read — may be capturable from app logs)
